summary:
The Western Phase of Polynesian Expansion (c. 500–200 BC) was the period when the ancestors of the Polynesian peoples, having dispersed across the western Pacific through the Lapita culture, settled the Tonga-Samoa region and began the long population pause before their eventual expansion into the vast eastern Pacific — one of history's greatest feats of maritime navigation.

The Lapita culture (c. 1600–500 BC) had spread from the Bismarck Archipelago across Melanesia to reach Fiji, Tonga, and Samoa. In Tonga and Samoa, the proto-Polynesian culture crystallized as communities became more isolated from western Melanesia. Navigation using stars, ocean swells, birds, and cloud patterns — knowledge accumulated and transmitted across generations — enabled these seafarers to voyage across open ocean without instruments. The western phase represents consolidation and development of the cultural and navigational traditions that would later (roughly 200 BC – 1000 AD) enable expansion eastward to the Cook Islands, Society Islands, Marquesas, Hawaii, Easter Island, and ultimately New Zealand. The achievement of navigating the vast, largely island-free central Pacific to reach Hawaii (c. 300 AD) and then continuing to New Zealand (c. 1250 AD) represents the culmination of a tradition rooted in this period.
